title: AGENTS.md — using ggOceanMaps with an AI assistant
text: basemap() returns regular ggplot object. map projected (polar custom CRS), decimal-degree data must transformed first — otherwise points land wrong place: unprojected (decimal-degree) maps can use geom_point() directly x = lon, y = lat.
code: # Use transform_coord() to project decimal-degree data dt <- transform_coord(my_df, bind = TRUE)   # adds lon.proj / lat.proj basemap(data = my_df) +   geom_point(data = dt, aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj))  # Or use ggspatial::geom_spatial_*, which handles the transform internally basemap(limits = c(-20, 30, 50, 70)) +   ggspatial::geom_spatial_point(data = my_df, aes(x = lon, y = lat),                                 crs = 4326)

title: AGENTS.md — using ggOceanMaps with an AI assistant
text: limits order c(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax), min/max pairs. c(-20, 30, 50, 70) “lon −20 30, lat 50 70”. single number means polar projection bounded latitude. Projected maps need transform_coord() added data. Polar maps custom-CRS maps decimal degrees. Using geom_point(aes(x = lon, y = lat)) polar basemap places points wrong location. transform_coord(data, bind = TRUE) adds lon.proj lat.proj columns; plot . use ggspatial::geom_spatial_point(). Antimeridian crossing: limits like c(160, -160, 60, 80) work, use rotate = TRUE get sensible-looking map. Without rotate, package prints message shows whole world. Bathymetry styles need data. bathy.style = "rcb", "pb", "cb" require ggOceanMapsLargeData download (options(ggOceanMaps.datapath)). "rub" / "rug" require options(ggOceanMaps.userpath = "path//file.nc"). default bathymetry = TRUE uses bundled binned raster works without download. Don’t add + coord_sf() manually. basemap() already sets coord_sf() right projection limits. Adding another reset package usually clip map incorrectly. shapefiles vs shape: shapefiles (plural) selects underlying spatial dataset ("Arctic", "Europe", …). shape ggplot2 aesthetic marker shape. Easy confuse. First call may take . bathymetry = TRUE triggers download, first basemap slow. Subsequent calls reuse cached data.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: basemap() (R/basemap.R) main user-facing function. follows pipeline: Determine bathy style — parses bathy.style string internal command name via define_bathy_style() Detects input “case” (polar limits, decimal degree limits, projected limits, sf/sp data, etc.) via basemap_data_detect_case() Resolves pre-made shapefile set use via define_shapefiles() / shapefile_list() Loads/downloads spatial data via load_map_data() Clips shapefiles map extent via basemap_data_crop() Computes grid line geometries via basemap_define_grid_lines() Assemble ggplot2 layers — map_cmd() (R/map_cmd.R) returns ggplot2 command strings element (base, land, glaciers, bathymetry, grid definitions). concatenated + executed via eval(parse(text = layers)). Annotate output — returned ggplot object gets custom attributes (bathymetry, glaciers, limits, polarmap, crs, etc.) attached downstream use. qmap() (R/qmap.R) thin wrapper basemap() quickly plotting data frame’s coordinates.

Recent antimeridian note: explicit rotated antimeridian/wide-span maps clipped projected map CRS, transforming projected clip box back WGS84 intersecting . title: Adding graphical elements
text: Barents-Sea-currents repository provides publication-style “Figure 1” current arrows Barents Sea North Atlantic. example downloads tidy Barents Sea CSV, builds one line per arrow, smooths , draws geom_path(). Smoothing happens projected metres rather longitude/latitude, exaggerate curves near pole. arrow group ordered nodes. Assemble one LINESTRING per arrow, reproject map CRS, smooth nodes curves smoothr::smooth(). arrows sit top basemap, otherwise drawn land. reorder_layers() pushes land, glacier, grid layers back top, tucking arrows coastline.
code: barents_url <- paste0(   "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/MikkoVihtakari/",   "Barents-Sea-currents/master/tabular/barents_currents.csv" ) barents_file <- file.path(tempdir(), "barents_currents.csv")  if (!file.exists(barents_file)) {   try(     download.file(barents_url, barents_file, quiet = TRUE, mode = "wb"),     silent = TRUE   ) }  # Offline fallback bundled with the vignette. if (!file.exists(barents_file)) {   barents_file <- file.path("data", "barents_currents.csv") }  cur <- read.csv(barents_file) head(cur) #>   id      long      lat order        group size     type #> 1  0  5.306694 66.34752     1 Atlantic_0.1    5 Atlantic #> 2  0  5.306694 66.34752     2 Atlantic_0.1    5 Atlantic #> 3  0  6.448918 66.73568     3 Atlantic_0.1    5 Atlantic #> 4  0  9.080173 67.53121     4 Atlantic_0.1    5 Atlantic #> 5  0 10.656624 68.29049     5 Atlantic_0.1    5 Atlantic #> 6  0 13.568487 69.01010     6 Atlantic_0.1    5 Atlantic library(sf)  cur <- cur[order(cur$group, cur$order), ] parts <- split(cur, cur$group)  arrows <- st_sf(   do.call(rbind, lapply(parts, function(d) d[1, c("group", "type", "size")])),   geometry = st_sfc(     lapply(parts, function(d) st_linestring(as.matrix(d[, c("long", "lat")]))),     crs = 4326   ) ) arrows <- smoothr::smooth(st_transform(arrows, 32633), method = "spline")  # Tidy projected coordinates for geom_path(). co <- as.data.frame(st_coordinates(arrows)) cur_lines <- data.frame(   x = co$X,   y = co$Y,   group = arrows$group[co$L1],   type = arrows$type[co$L1],   size = arrows$size[co$L1] ) current_arrow <- arrow(   type = "open",   angle = 15,   ends = "last",   length = unit(0.25, "lines") )  reorder_layers(   basemap(     limits = c(5, 45, 68, 83.5),     crs = 32633,     bathy.style = "rbg",     land.col = "grey86",     legends = c(FALSE, TRUE)   ) +     geom_path(       data = cur_lines,       aes(x, y, group = group, colour = type, linewidth = size),       arrow = current_arrow     ) +     scale_colour_manual(       "Current type",       values = c(Atlantic = "#d7301f", Arctic = "#2166ac"),       labels = c(Atlantic = "Atlantic (warm)", Arctic = "Arctic (cold)")     ) +     scale_linewidth("Relative strength", range = c(0.3, 1.3)) )

title: Adding graphical elements
text: gridded velocity field, compute arrow endpoints, transform start end points basemap CRS, draw result geom_segment(). example uses small synthetic field filtered ocean positions; pattern applies reading u v NetCDF file.  decimal-degree map code works without transform_coord() calls. Use maps basemap EPSG:4326; many high-latitude limits select polar projection automatically.
code: grd <- expand.grid(lon = seq(-14, 6, by = 4), lat = seq(57, 69, by = 3)) grd <- dist2land(grd, binary = TRUE, dist.col = "ocean", verbose = FALSE) grd <- grd[grd$ocean, ]  grd$u <- 0.35 + 0.25 * cos((grd$lat - 58) / 4) # eastward component grd$v <- 0.20 * sin((grd$lon + 5) / 6) # northward component  # Display scale only: a 1 m/s vector is drawn as 50 hours of drift. km_per_ms <- 50 * 3.6 grd$lon_end <- grd$lon +   (grd$u * km_per_ms) / (111.32 * cos(grd$lat * pi / 180)) grd$lat_end <- grd$lat + (grd$v * km_per_ms) / 110.57  # Transform both ends to the basemap projection. start <- transform_coord(   grd,   lon = "lon",   lat = "lat",   proj.out = 3995,   bind = TRUE ) end <- transform_coord(   grd,   lon = "lon_end",   lat = "lat_end",   proj.out = 3995,   new.names = c("lon_end.proj", "lat_end.proj") ) grd <- cbind(start, end)  basemap(   limits = c(-20, 30, 50, 70),   bathy.style = "rbg",   land.col = "grey86",   legends = FALSE ) +   geom_segment(     data = grd,     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, xend = lon_end.proj, yend = lat_end.proj),     arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.12, "cm"), type = "open"),     colour = "#084d9a",     linewidth = 0.45   )

title: Adding graphical elements
text: Pie charts can drawn ordinary polygons. avoids extra plotting dependency keeps coordinate units explicit: projection pie radius metres. helper turns row category counts one polygon per slice.  basemap already maps bathymetry fill, insert ggnewscale::new_scale_fill() basemap pie layer give slices independent fill scale.
code: pie_polygons <- function(data, cols, x, y, r, n = 60) {   out <- vector("list", nrow(data) * length(cols))   k <- 1    for (i in seq_len(nrow(data))) {     values <- as.numeric(data[i, cols])     values[is.na(values)] <- 0     prop <- values / sum(values)     starts <- c(0, cumsum(prop)[-length(prop)]) * 2 * pi     ends <- cumsum(prop) * 2 * pi      for (j in seq_along(cols)) {       theta <- seq(         starts[j],         ends[j],         length.out = max(2, ceiling(n * prop[j]))       )       out[[k]] <- data.frame(         id = data$id[i],         slice = cols[j],         x = c(           data[[x]][i],           data[[x]][i] + cos(theta) * data[[r]][i],           data[[x]][i]         ),         y = c(           data[[y]][i],           data[[y]][i] + sin(theta) * data[[r]][i],           data[[y]][i]         ),         part = paste(data$id[i], cols[j], sep = "_")       )       k <- k + 1     }   }    do.call(rbind, out) } pies <- data.frame(   id = c("A", "B", "C", "D"),   lon = c(-8, 0, 4, 9),   lat = c(56.5, 60, 63.5, 67.5),   cod = c(45, 20, 60, 25),   haddock = c(30, 50, 15, 35),   saithe = c(25, 30, 25, 40) ) slices <- c("cod", "haddock", "saithe")  pies <- transform_coord(pies, proj.out = 3995, bind = TRUE) pies$r <- 90000 # metres pie_df <- pie_polygons(pies, slices, x = "lon.proj", y = "lat.proj", r = "r")  basemap(   limits = c(-12, 16, 54, 70),   bathy.style = "rbg",   land.col = "grey86",   legends = FALSE ) +   ggnewscale::new_scale_fill() +   geom_polygon(     data = pie_df,     aes(x = x, y = y, group = part, fill = slice),     colour = "white",     linewidth = 0.15,     alpha = 0.95   ) +   scale_fill_brewer("Species", palette = "Set2")

title: Adding graphical elements
text: scales recolour bathymetry ggOceanMaps already prepared. bin raw depth raster classes , re-bin raster_bathymetry(): pass cut points depths hand returned binned raster straight basemap() shapefiles. grid stars::read_stars() can open works — example reads local GEBCO file (see raster section Bathymetry article), cropped shelf Lofoten. standard grey land comes coarser global coastline, can sit slightly high-resolution grid like GEBCO. Pull land raster vector_land() coastline matches bathymetry exactly — estimate.land = TRUE kept land cells . Drop "land" class raster drawn depth bin. binned raster behaves like binned bathymetry: switch bathymetry = TRUE recolour discrete fill scale (sequential ColorBrewer palette ). raster_bathymetry() reports intervals rb$depth.invervals. binned raster can turned depth-contour polygons vector_bathymetry() pre-made-shapefile workflow described Customising shapefiles article.
code: # Your local GEBCO/ETOPO/IBCAO grid, e.g. set once with # options(ggOceanMaps.userpath = "path/to/GEBCO_2025.nc") gebco <- getOption("ggOceanMaps.userpath")  rb <- raster_bathymetry(   gebco,   depths = c(50, 100, 250, 500, 1000, 2000), # depth class boundaries   boundary = c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), # crop to the map region   estimate.land = TRUE, # keep land cells so vector_land() can use them   verbose = FALSE )  # Each depths value becomes a class boundary. levels(rb$raster[[1]]) # Land from the same grid lines up with the bathymetry. land <- vector_land(rb) land <- sf::st_transform(land, 4326) # normalise GEBCO's "unknown" CRS  # Drop the land class so the raster shows depth bins only. v <- rb$raster[[1]] v[as.character(v) == "land"] <- NA rb$raster[[1]] <- droplevels(v)  basemap(   limits = c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6),   shapefiles = list(land = land, glacier = NULL, bathy = rb),   bathymetry = TRUE,   grid.col = NA ) +   scale_fill_brewer("Depth (m)", palette = "YlGnBu", na.value = "white")

title: Bathymetry in ggOceanMaps
text: companion repository ggOceanMapsLargeData hosts higher-resolution data supported regions (Decimal Degree, Arctic Stereographic, Antarctic Stereographic, several pre-made regional shapefile sets). provides three styles: continuous raster, filled polygon contours, contour lines. basemap() downloads needs first use caches locally. One-time setup. Choose permanent directory files persist R sessions, add line ~/.Rprofile: first call region prompt asks confirm download (roughly 15–100 MB depending region). Subsequent calls read cache immediate.

title: Bathymetry in ggOceanMaps
text: Decimal-degree limits . Polar maps projected-CRS limits yet supported. Per-source size caps. EMODnet defaults 50 deg² maximum bounding box (reads 8-byte doubles internally, 4° tile already exceeds read cap); ETOPO defaults 2000 deg² underlying grid much coarser. Override either max_area_deg2 = ... wcs_bathymetry(). Larger boxes tiled mosaicked automatically.

Use downsample trade resolution speed. ggOceanMaps.userpath also used get_depth() look point depths raster:
code: # Set once (in .Rprofile for persistence): options(ggOceanMaps.userpath = "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc")  # Then any basemap call uses your file: basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rub") basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rub", downsample = 10) basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rug") # greyscale dt <- data.frame(lon = seq(-20, 80, length.out = 41), lat = 50:90) dt <- get_depth(dt, bathy.style = "ru") qmap(dt, color = depth) + scale_color_viridis_c()

title: Bathymetry in ggOceanMaps
text: need contour polygons, custom depth bins, matched land + bathymetry pair, raster_bathymetry() / vector_bathymetry() / vector_land() pipeline turns raster source reusable shapefile objects: Setting depths = NULL skips binning step returns continuous raster — useful geom_raster-style fills without vectorization overhead. Save processed objects processing cost paid :
code: # 1. Process the raster — crop, sign-flip, optionally bin into contours rb <- raster_bathymetry(   "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc",   depths = c(50, 200, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000),  # depth break points   boundary = c(-5, 10, 50, 60)                  # crop to your region )  # 2a. Vectorize bathymetry into depth-band polygons vb <- vector_bathymetry(rb, drop.crumbs = 10)   # drop islands < 10 km²  # 2b. Vectorize land from the same raster vl <- vector_land(rb, drop.crumbs = 10)  # 3. Plot — both layers plug into basemap() basemap(c(-5, 10, 50, 60),         shapefiles = list(land = vl, glacier = NULL, bathy = vb),         bathymetry = TRUE) save(vb, vl, file = "my_region_bathy.rda")

what: How do I use my own bathymetry raster (e.g. GEBCO, ETOPO, IBCAO)?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: Download grid source (GEBCO, ETOPO 2022) save NetCDF (.nc) GeoTIFF (.tif). Quick route — point ggOceanMaps.userpath file: basemap() crops plot extent, sign-flips (negative depths → positive), renders raster automatically. Use "raster_user_greys" / "rug" greyscale. downsample argument reduces resolution native grid fine plot size. Manual route — pre-process full control: manual route also entry point vectorizing bathymetry (depth contour polygons) — see Building shapefiles section .
code: options(ggOceanMaps.userpath = "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc")  basemap(limits = c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "raster_user_blues") # or the abbreviation: basemap(limits = c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rub") rb <- raster_bathymetry(   "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc",   depths = NULL, # continuous raster; use a depth vector for polygons   boundary = c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6) # crop early to save memory )  # A raw continuous raster needs an explicit continuous style ("rcb"); the # bathymetry = TRUE default assumes pre-binned data like dd_rbathy. basemap(   limits = c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6),   shapefiles = list(land = dd_land, glacier = NULL, bathy = rb$raster),   bathy.style = "rcb" )

what: How do I fetch bathymetry on demand from a web service?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: Two WCS sources wired basemap() — pick region: manual fetch (e.g. pass vector_bathymetry() custom shapefile workflow), call wcs_bathymetry() directly. user-raster route , raw fetched raster continuous, pair explicit bathy.style = "rcb" rather bathymetry = TRUE: Requirements / caveats: Decimal-degree limits ; polar maps supported. EMODnet covers European regional seas (≈−36° 43° lon, ≈15° 90° lat). Outside , use ETOPO. ggOceanMaps error clear message suggested alternative pick wrong source. Bbox size capped per source: 50°² EMODnet (~115 m grid), 2000°² ETOPO (~1.85 km grid). Override max_area_deg2 larger requests; function tiles automatically. Caching uses getOption("ggOceanMaps.datapath") (tempdir() fallback). Set permanently .Rprofile cross-session reuse. Citation: EMODnet CC-(https://emodnet.ec.europa.eu/en/bathymetry); ETOPO1 Amante & Eakins 2009, NOAA NGDC (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/etopo-global-relief-model).
code: # North Sea — high resolution from EMODnet basemap(c(2, 3, 54, 55), bathy.style = "wemb") # Hawaii — global coverage from ETOPO basemap(c(-160, -154, 18, 23), bathy.style = "wceb") # Indonesia / Java Trench — also ETOPO (outside EMODnet) basemap(c(100, 130, -15, 10), bathy.style = "wceb") bathy <- wcs_bathymetry(c(2, 3, 54, 55), source = "emodnet")  basemap(   c(2, 3, 54, 55),   shapefiles = list(land = dd_land, glacier = NULL, bathy = bathy$raster),   bathy.style = "rcb" )

what: How do I make land + bathymetry from a downloaded raster?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: full pipeline single source raster (GEBCO, ETOPO, IBCAO, …) matched land + bathymetry pair : Use depths = NULL instead depth vector keep raster continuous grid (skips vectorization, faster rendering). Pass result straight basemap() bathy shapefile, explicit continuous style: Tip: save processed objects .rda files don’t re-process full raster every session:
code: library(ggOceanMaps)  north_sea <- c(-5, 10, 50, 60)  # 1. Process the raster — crop, sign-flip, bin depth levels. # drop.crumbs is left NULL: vector_bathymetry()/vector_land() each apply it # independently, so a shared non-NULL threshold can drop a small island from # one layer without dropping the matching sliver of shallow water from the # other, leaving tiny gaps right at the coast. rb <- raster_bathymetry(   "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc",   depths = c(20, 50, 100, 200, 500), # depth contour break points   boundary = north_sea,   estimate.land = TRUE # keep land cells for vector_land() )  # 2a. Vectorize bathymetry to depth-polygon shapefile vb <- vector_bathymetry(rb, drop.crumbs = NULL)  # 2b. Vectorize land from the same raster vl <- vector_land(rb, drop.crumbs = NULL)  # 2c. GEBCO/ETOPO/IBCAO grids are read with a non-standard "unknown" CRS # (axis order lat/lon instead of lon/lat), which slants the clip box if left # as-is. Normalise both layers to plain EPSG:4326 before plotting. vb <- sf::st_transform(vb, 4326) vl <- sf::st_transform(vl, 4326)  # estimate.land = TRUE adds a "land" class to vb -- drop it, it belongs in vl. vb <- vb[vb$depth != "land", ] vb$depth <- droplevels(vb$depth)  # 3. Plot — plug both into basemap(). Vector bathymetry needs an explicit # bathy.style ("pb" here); bathymetry = TRUE alone assumes a raster. basemap(   limits = north_sea,   shapefiles = list(land = vl, glacier = NULL, bathy = vb),   bathy.style = "pb" ) rb_cont <- raster_bathymetry(   "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc",   depths = NULL,   boundary = north_sea )  basemap(   limits = north_sea,   shapefiles = list(land = dd_land, glacier = NULL, bathy = rb_cont$raster),   bathy.style = "rcb" ) save(vb, vl, file = "north_sea_bathy.rda")

what: How do I clip an existing shapefile to my region?

what: How do I draw ocean current vectors over a map?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: pattern : get u/v current components grid, thin drawable density, transform coordinates map’s CRS, draw arrows geom_segment(). example builds small synthetic field dist2land() filtering land points, fully self-contained; pattern applies reading real u/v components NetCDF file stars::read_stars(). transform_coord() needed even though limits look like ordinary decimal-degree box: latitudes far north (50–70°N) select Arctic polar projection automatically (see ?basemap), coord_sf() still labels axes plain degrees, projection easy miss (see Adding graphical elements article pattern):  explicitly polar map pattern identical — region projection differ. example moves north Arctic Ocean uses synthetic zonal (eastward) flow, traces clean circumpolar gyre around pole; bathy.style = "poly_greys" keeps background quiet arrows stand :
code: grd <- expand.grid(lon = seq(-14, 6, by = 4), lat = seq(57, 69, by = 3)) grd <- dist2land(grd, binary = TRUE, dist.col = "ocean", verbose = FALSE) grd <- grd[grd$ocean, ] # drop points on land  grd$u <- 0.35 + 0.25 * cos((grd$lat - 58) / 4) # eastward component grd$v <- 0.20 * sin((grd$lon + 5) / 6) # northward component  # Display scale only: a 1 m/s vector is drawn as 50 hours of drift. km_per_ms <- 50 * 3.6 grd$lon_end <- grd$lon +   (grd$u * km_per_ms) / (111.32 * cos(grd$lat * pi / 180)) grd$lat_end <- grd$lat + (grd$v * km_per_ms) / 110.57  # Transform both ends of each arrow to the basemap's actual CRS. start <- transform_coord(   grd,   lon = "lon",   lat = "lat",   proj.out = 3995,   bind = TRUE ) end <- transform_coord(   grd,   lon = "lon_end",   lat = "lat_end",   proj.out = 3995,   new.names = c("lon_end.proj", "lat_end.proj") ) grd <- cbind(start, end)  basemap(   limits = c(-20, 30, 50, 70),   bathy.style = "rbg",   land.col = "grey86",   legends = FALSE ) +   geom_segment(     data = grd,     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, xend = lon_end.proj, yend = lat_end.proj),     arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.12, "cm"), type = "open"),     colour = "#084d9a",     linewidth = 0.45   ) grd <- expand.grid(lon = seq(-180, 165, by = 15), lat = seq(81, 87, by = 3)) grd <- dist2land(grd, binary = TRUE, dist.col = "ocean", verbose = FALSE) grd <- grd[grd$ocean, ]  grd$u <- 0.4 # pure eastward flow traces a circle around the pole grd$v <- 0  km_per_ms <- 80 * 3.6 grd$lon_end <- grd$lon +   (grd$u * km_per_ms) / (111.32 * cos(grd$lat * pi / 180)) grd$lat_end <- grd$lat + (grd$v * km_per_ms) / 110.57  start <- transform_coord(   grd,   lon = "lon",   lat = "lat",   proj.out = 3995,   bind = TRUE ) end <- transform_coord(   grd,   lon = "lon_end",   lat = "lat_end",   proj.out = 3995,   new.names = c("lon_end.proj", "lat_end.proj") ) grd <- cbind(start, end)  basemap(75, bathy.style = "poly_greys") +   geom_segment(     data = grd,     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, xend = lon_end.proj, yend = lat_end.proj),     arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")),     color = "#0c5ba0",     linewidth = 0.5   )

what: How do I add ICES or Norwegian fishery zones?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: ggOceanMaps ships three zone datasets: three sf polygon objects can passed straight basemap() automatic limits overlaid geom_sf():   Label columns: ices_areas$Area_Full (full area name), ices_areas$SubArea, ices_areas$Major_FA; fdir_main_areas$main_area; fdir_sub_areas$main_area, fdir_sub_areas$sub_area.
code: # Fit map to the extent of the ICES areas and draw them as red outlines p <- basemap(ices_areas) +   geom_sf(data = ices_areas, fill = NA, color = "red", linewidth = 0.4)  # reorder_layers() pushes land/glacier/grid back on top of the outlines, and # geom_sf_text() labels each area at its centroid (Area_Full's "27." prefix # is common to all ICES areas, so it is stripped for shorter labels). reorder_layers(p) +   geom_sf_text(     data = suppressWarnings(sf::st_centroid(sf::st_make_valid(ices_areas))),     aes(label = gsub("27.", "", Area_Full)),     size = FS(8),     fontface = 2,     color = "red"   ) # Norwegian fisheries zones with filled polygons basemap(fdir_main_areas, bathymetry = TRUE) +   geom_sf(data = fdir_main_areas, fill = NA, color = "orange") +   geom_sf_label(data = fdir_main_areas, aes(label = main_area))

what: How do I add longitude/latitude labels to a polar map?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: Polar maps need manual labels. Pull grid lines map’s attributes, keep one meridian/parallel, project label positions, draw :
code: p <- basemap(-60, shapefiles = "Antarctic")  # Latitude labels along the 180° meridian lat_lab <- as.data.frame(   sf::st_coordinates(attributes(p)$map.grid$lat.grid.lines)) lat_lab <- lat_lab[lat_lab$X == -180, ] lat_lab <- rbind(lat_lab, data.frame(X = -180, Y = -90, L1 = 3, L2 = 1)) lat_lab <- transform_coord(lat_lab, proj.out = attributes(p)$crs, bind = TRUE)  # Longitude labels just outside the -60° parallel lon_lab <- as.data.frame(   sf::st_coordinates(attributes(p)$map.grid$lon.grid.lines)) lon_lab <- lon_lab[lon_lab$Y == -60, ] lon_lab$Y <- -63 lon_lab <- transform_coord(lon_lab, proj.out = attributes(p)$crs, bind = TRUE)  p +   geom_text(data = lat_lab,             aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, label = paste0(abs(Y), "\u00B0S"))) +   geom_text(data = lon_lab,             aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj,                 label = ifelse(sign(X) == 1, paste0(abs(X), "\u00B0E"),                                paste0(abs(X), "\u00B0W")))) +   theme(plot.margin = margin(0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25, "cm"))

title: Customising shapefiles
text: basemap() accepts named list three sf/stars objects: land — sf polygon layer (required). glacier — sf polygon layer, NULL none. bathy — either stars raster (continuous/binned raster styles) sf polygon layer depth contours (vector styles), NULL. three must share one projection. basemap() uses CRS map, need call coord_sf() . shapefile_list() shows structure built-sets good template.
code: basemap(   limits = c(-5, 10, 50, 60),   shapefiles = list(land = my_land, glacier = NULL, bathy = my_bathy),   bathymetry = TRUE )

title: Customising shapefiles
text: clip_shapefile() crops sf polygon layer bounding box. quickest way make regional land layer larger one (e.g. shipped dd_land): limits interpreted proj.limits (decimal degrees default). projected input layer, pass limits projection’s units set proj.limits CRS, keep limits degrees let function reproject clip box. Use return.boundary = TRUE get clipping polygon back inspection.
code: land <- clip_shapefile(   dd_land,   limits = c(-5, 10, 50, 60) # c(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax), decimal degrees )  basemap(shapefiles = list(land = land, glacier = NULL, bathy = NULL))

what: Building land + bathymetry from a raster
title: Customising shapefiles
text: single source grid (GEBCO, ETOPO, IBCAO, …) gives matched land bathymetry pair. pipeline raster_bathymetry() first, vector_bathymetry() /vector_land(). example reuses North Sea region clipping example , depth breaks suited mostly-shallow shelf deeper Norwegian Trench along Norwegian coast: raster_bathymetry() returns bathyRaster: list processed raster (stars object) depth intervals. Feed object vectorisers: Normalise CRS (step 2c). GEBCO, ETOPO IBCAO NetCDF grids read non-standard “unknown” geographic CRS — unnamed datum latitude/longitude (rather longitude/latitude) axis order. basemap() clips renders map land layer’s CRS, axis-swapped CRS makes rectangular clip box slant slightly, leaving thin uncovered wedge along southern map edge. Transforming vb vl plain EPSG:4326 plotting removes . vectorised layers, via proj.raster_bathymetry(): reprojecting raster turns curvilinear grid vector_bathymetry() polygonise. vector_land() takes bathyRaster vector_bathymetry() cleanup arguments (drop.crumbs, remove.holes, smooth). Leave drop.crumbs NULL need vl vb share coastline: two functions apply independently, shared non-NULL threshold can drop small island one layer without dropping matching sliver shallow water , leaving tiny gaps coast. drop.crumbs still useful shrink file size don’t need two layers line exactly — km², scale small regions; city-block-scale area needs something like 0.01, 10. keep continuous (unbinned) raster bathymetry instead depth-contour polygons, pass depths = NULL raster_bathymetry() use rb$raster directly bathy (skipping vector_bathymetry()). Save processed layers re-run heavy raster step every session:
code: # 1. Read + crop + sign-flip the grid; bin to depth contours. rb <- raster_bathymetry(   bathy = "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc",   depths = c(20, 50, 100, 200, 500), # contour break points   boundary = c(-5, 10, 50, 60), # crop early to save memory   estimate.land = TRUE # keep land cells for vector_land() ) # 2a. Depth-contour polygons for the vector bathymetry styles vb <- vector_bathymetry(rb, drop.crumbs = NULL)  # 2b. Land polygons extracted from the same grid vl <- vector_land(rb, drop.crumbs = NULL)  # 2c. Normalise the CRS to plain EPSG:4326 (see note below). vb <- sf::st_transform(vb, 4326) vl <- sf::st_transform(vl, 4326)  # vector_bathymetry() also polygonizes the "land" class added by # estimate.land -- drop it, it belongs in vl. vb <- vb[vb$depth != "land", ] vb$depth <- droplevels(vb$depth)  # 3. Plug both into basemap(). Vector bathymetry needs an explicit # bathy.style ("pb" here); bathymetry = TRUE alone assumes a raster. basemap(   limits = c(-5, 10, 50, 60),   shapefiles = list(land = vl, glacier = NULL, bathy = vb),   bathy.style = "pb" ) save(vb, vl, file = "north_sea_shapes.rda") # later: load("north_sea_shapes.rda")

what: Pairing a WCS fetch with a premade shapefile set
title: Customising shapefiles
text: wcs_bathymetry() fetches bathymetry demand Web Coverage Service returns continuous bathyRaster, always discards land (calls raster_bathymetry(ras, depths = NULL) internally). Since section making new shapefiles rather just plotting raster, example vectorises fetched bathymetry depth-contour polygons — cut() + vector_bathymetry() pattern raster section — pairs premade "Europe" land set, better match EMODnet’s ~115 m resolution shipped dd_land: Fetch little wider display. map plotted "Europe" land set’s CRS (EPSG:3035), WCS request decimal-degree (EPSG:4326) box. straight lat/lon edges slant reprojected 3035, fetch box exactly equal map limits leaves uncovered slivers projected map’s corners. Requesting slightly larger fetch box tromso area display covers whole panel. Clipping land st_bbox(vb) keeps projection bathymetry map, clipping happens plotted CRS rather across reprojection. load_map_data() internal helper basemap() uses load , needed, download premade set’s layers — calling directly gets just "Europe" land polygons without default bathymetry. quick look raw WCS fetch without vectorising, pair bathy$raster directly dd_land use bathymetry = TRUE instead, wcs_bathymetry()’s examples.
code: tromso <- c(18, 20, 69.4, 69.9) # area to display fetch <- c(17.5, 20.5, 69.3, 70) # slightly wider fetch box (see note below) bathy <- wcs_bathymetry(fetch, source = "emodnet")  # wcs_bathymetry() always returns depths = NULL (continuous), so bin it # into depth-contour classes by hand before vectorising. breaks <- c(-Inf, 20, 50, 100, 200, Inf) labels <- c("0-20", "20-50", "50-100", "100-200", "200-Inf") rb <- list(raster = cut(bathy$raster, breaks, labels = labels)) class(rb) <- "bathyRaster" vb <- vector_bathymetry(rb, drop.crumbs = NULL)  # Reproject the bathymetry to the "Europe" land set's CRS (EPSG:3035) and clip # the land to the bathymetry extent, so both are in the plotted projection. europe_land <- load_map_data(shapefile_list("Europe"))$land vb <- sf::st_transform(vb, sf::st_crs(europe_land)) land <- sf::st_crop(sf::st_make_valid(europe_land), sf::st_bbox(vb))  basemap(   limits = tromso,   shapefiles = list(land = land, glacier = NULL, bathy = vb),   bathy.style = "pb" )

title: SI2022 poster
text: Script download data used poster: Note correct way download something like shown website, user name making poster, <7000 records shark species Northeast Atlantic. Hence 100k limit issue. Citing correct data sources, however, issue planning use data serious applications posters, make sure find correct DOI using occ_download() web portal.
code: # GBIF data  library(tidyverse) library(ggOceanMaps) library(rgbif)  ## Donwload example from: https://docs.ropensci.org/rgbif  species <- c(BlueShark = "Prionace glauca", BaskingShark = "Cetorhinus maximus")  GBIF <- lapply(seq_along(species), function(i) {      message(species[i], "...")      key <- rgbif::name_backbone(name = species[[i]])[c("canonicalName", "speciesKey")]      rgbif::occ_search(     taxonKey = key$speciesKey,      return = "data",      limit = 4e5,      geometry = c(-20, 30, 20, 76)) })  GBIF_blue <- GBIF[[1]]$data %>%    rename("lat" = "decimalLatitude", "lon" = "decimalLongitude") %>%    dplyr::select(lon, lat) %>%    transform_coord(bind = TRUE) %>%    dist2land()  GBIF_basking <- GBIF[[2]]$data %>%    rename("lat" = "decimalLatitude", "lon" = "decimalLongitude") %>%    dplyr::select(lon, lat) %>%    transform_coord(bind = TRUE) %>%    dist2land()  # Dugnad for havet data  DugnadForHavet <- read_delim(   "data/DFH_basking_shark.csv", # from: https://dugnadforhavet.no/dataportal   delim = ";") %>%    arrange(amount) %>%    filter(!is.na(longitude), !is.na(latitude)) %>%    transform_coord(bind = TRUE) %>%    dist2land() %>%    as_tibble() keys <- species %>%    name_backbone_checklist() %>% # match to backbone    filter(!matchType == "NONE") %>% # get matched names   pull(usageKey)  occ_download(   pred_in("taxonKey",key$speciesKey),   pred("hasCoordinate", TRUE),   pred("hasGeospatialIssue", FALSE),   pred_within("POLYGON ((-20 30, 20 30, 20 80, -20 80, -20 30))") )

title: SI2022 poster
code: ## Background map  a1_h <- 841-1 # Poster height without margins (A1) a1_w <- 594-1.2 # Poster width without margins (A1)  asp <- a1_h/a1_w # To get correct size because aspect ratio is fixed lon_min <- -1.5e6 # Projected coordinates lon_max <- 2e6  lat_min <- -6.5e6 lat_max <- lat_min + asp*diff(c(lon_min, lon_max))  bm <-    basemap(     c(lon_min, lon_max, lat_min, lat_max),      shapefiles = "Arctic",     lon.interval = 10,     bathymetry = TRUE,     legends = FALSE) +   theme(axis.title = element_blank()) +   geom_spatial_point(     data = DugnadForHavet,     aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, size = amount),     color = "#FF5F68", alpha = 0.8   ) +   geom_density_2d(     data = DugnadForHavet, contour_var = "ndensity", size = LS(1),     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, color = after_stat(level))   ) +   geom_spatial_point(     data = GBIF_blue %>% filter(ldist > 0),      aes(x = lon, y = lat), color = "#FFC95B", alpha = 0.8, size = 3   ) +   geom_density_2d(     data = GBIF_blue, contour_var = "ndensity", size = LS(1),     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, color = after_stat(level))   ) +   scale_size(range = c(1,10)) +   scale_color_distiller(palette = "Spectral", na.value = NA) +   theme(legend.position = "none")  ## Distance to land  DugnadForHavet %>%    mutate(species = "Basking shark") %>%    dplyr::select(species, ldist) %>%    bind_rows(     GBIF_basking %>% mutate(species = "Basking shark") %>%        dplyr::select(species, ldist),     GBIF_blue %>%        mutate(species = "Blue shark") %>%        dplyr::select(species, ldist)   ) %>%    ggplot(aes(x = ldist, after_stat(ndensity), color = species)) +    geom_freqpoly(size = LS(5)) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("#FF5F68", "#FFC95B")) +   labs(x = "Standardized density", y = "Distance from land (km)",         color = "Species") +   coord_cartesian(expand = FALSE, ylim = c(0,1.05), xlim = c(0,500)) +   theme_classic() +   theme(legend.position = c(0.7,0.9))

code: etopoPath <- "path/to/etopo" NEDPath   <- "path/to/naturalearth" gebcoPath <- "path/to/gebco" EEAPath   <- "path/to/eea" outPath   <- "path/to/output" arcticCRS    <- sf::st_crs(shapefile_list("Arctic")$crs) antarcticCRS <- sf::st_crs(shapefile_list("Antarctic")$crs) ddCRS        <- sf::st_crs(shapefile_list("Decimal")$crs)

title: Pre-made shapefiles
text: Bathymetry — binned raster shipped package continuous version: Land — Natural Earth land + minor islands. Natural Earth polygons require s2 engine turned clipping: Glaciers — Natural Earth glaciated areas + Antarctic ice shelves:
code: # Binned raster bathymetry (shipped as dd_rbathy) dd_rbathy <- raster_bathymetry(   bathy = file.path(etopoPath, "ETOPO_2022_v1_60s_N90W180_surface.nc"),   depths = c(50, 300, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 4000, 6000, 10000),   proj.out = 4326,   downsample = 1 ) save(dd_rbathy, file = file.path(outPath, "dd_rbathy.rda"), compress = "xz")  # Continuous raster bathymetry dd_rbathy_cont <- raster_bathymetry(   bathy = file.path(etopoPath, "ETOPO_2022_v1_60s_N90W180_surface.nc"),   depths = NULL,   proj.out = 4326,   downsample = 1 ) save(dd_rbathy_cont, file = file.path(outPath, "dd_rbathy_cont.rda"), compress = "xz") s2_mode <- sf::sf_use_s2() suppressMessages(sf::sf_use_s2(FALSE)) on.exit(suppressMessages(sf::sf_use_s2(s2_mode)))  world   <- sf::st_read(file.path(NEDPath, "ne_10m_land/ne_10m_land.shp")) islands <- sf::st_read(file.path(NEDPath, "ne_10m_minor_islands/ne_10m_minor_islands.shp"))  world   <- sf::st_make_valid(rbind(world, islands)) dd_land <- clip_shapefile(world, c(-180, 180, -90, 90)) all(sf::st_is_valid(dd_land))  save(dd_land, file = file.path(outPath, "dd_land.rda"), compress = "xz") glaciers    <- sf::st_read(file.path(NEDPath, "ne_10m_glaciated_areas/ne_10m_glaciated_areas.shp")) iceshelves  <- sf::st_read(file.path(NEDPath, "ne_10m_antarctic_ice_shelves_polys/ne_10m_antarctic_ice_shelves_polys.shp"))  glaciers   <- sf::st_make_valid(rbind(glaciers, iceshelves)) dd_glacier <- clip_shapefile(glaciers, c(-180, 180, -90, 90)) all(sf::st_is_valid(dd_glacier))  save(dd_glacier, file = file.path(outPath, "dd_glacier.rda"), compress = "xz")

code: rb <- raster_bathymetry(   bathy = file.path(etopoPath, "ETOPO_2022_v1_60s_N90W180_surface.nc"),   depths = c(50, 300, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 4000, 6000, 10000),   proj.out = arcticCRS,   boundary = c(-180, 180, 1, 90),   downsample = 1 ) arctic_bathy <- vector_bathymetry(rb) save(arctic_bathy, file = file.path(outPath, "arctic_bathy.rda"), compress = "xz") arctic_land <- clip_shapefile(world, c(-180, 180, 1, 90)) arctic_land <- sf::st_make_valid(sf::st_transform(arctic_land, arcticCRS)) save(arctic_land, file = file.path(outPath, "arctic_land.rda"), compress = "xz")  arctic_glacier <- clip_shapefile(glaciers, c(-180, 180, 40, 90)) arctic_glacier <- sf::st_make_valid(sf::st_transform(arctic_glacier, arcticCRS)) save(arctic_glacier, file = file.path(outPath, "arctic_glacier.rda"), compress = "xz")

Using data limit map, making points barely fit map. expand.factor argument can used adjust space map borders points. limits given projected coordinates decimal degrees maps -60 < latitude < 60, limit elements represent lines encompassing map area cartesian space. Projections shapefiles specified, function uses either limits data arguments decide projection use. --date conditions defined define_shapefiles shapefile_list functions. time writing, function uses three different projections (given EPSG codes) 3995 WGS 84 / Arctic Polar Stereographic. Called "ArcticStereographic". max latitude (limits[4]) >= 60 (min latitude (limits[3]) >= 30), single integer latitudes >= 30 <= 89. 3031 WGS 84 / Antarctic Polar Stereographic. Called "AntarcticStereographic". max latitude (limits[4]) <= -60 (min latitude (limits[3]) <= -30), single integer latitudes <= -30 >= -89. 4326 WGS 84 / World Geodetic System 1984, used GPS. Called "DecimalDegree". min latitude (limits[3]) < 30 > -30, max latitude (limits[4]) < 60 > -60, single integer latitudes < 30 > -30. rotate argument changes pre-defined projection mid-longitude point map points northward. crs argument can used define projection, can useful plotting, instance, model data difficult transform another projection. Bathymetry Bathymetry can plotted simply specifying bathymetry = TRUE bathy.style. former uses low-resolution raster shipped ggOceanMaps; latter selects one styles listed . See dedicated bathymetry vignette full walk-examples. bathy.style string follows pattern geometry_palette, geometry encodes underlying data type (raster grid, polygon contours contour lines) data come (shipped, downloaded, user-supplied web service), palette either blues grays. styles short abbreviation. Substitute g b end abbreviation switch blues grays (e.g. rbb → rbg). Add suffix _grays (full alias) change last letter b → g (abbreviation) get greyscale variant style _blues version, e.g. raster_binned_grays (rbg), wcs_emodnet_grays (wemg), poly_grays (pg). bathy.style NULL (default), read getOption("ggOceanMaps.bathy.style") falls back "raster_binned_blues". Override globally options(ggOceanMaps.bathy.style = "...") .Rprofile (e.g. "poly_blues" mimic pre-2.0 ggOceanMaps). text: Mikko Vihtakari

title: Create a ggplot2 basemap for plotting variables — basemap
code: # The easiest way to produce a map is to use the limits # argument and decimal degrees:  basemap(limits = 60) # synonym to basemap(60)  # \donttest{ # Bathymetry can be added using the respective argument: basemap(limits = -60, bathymetry = TRUE)   if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Glaciers require a download in the new version: basemap(limits = -60, glaciers = TRUE, shapefiles = "Arctic") } # }  # The easiest way to add data on the maps is to use the ggspatial functions: dt <- data.frame(lon = c(-150, 150), lat = c(60, 90)) if(requireNamespace("ggspatial", quietly = TRUE)) { basemap(data = dt, bathymetry = TRUE) +   ggspatial::geom_spatial_point(data = dt, aes(x = lon, y = lat),     color = "red") } #> Assuming `crs = 4326` in stat_spatial_identity()  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Note that writing out data = dt is required because there are multiple # underlying ggplot layers plotted already: basemap(data = dt) + ggspatial::geom_spatial_point(dt, aes(x = lon, y = lat), color = "red") #> Error: `mapping` must be created by `aes()` } # }  # If you want to use native ggplot commands, you need to transform your data # to the projection used by the map: dt <- transform_coord(dt, bind = TRUE)  basemap(data = dt) +   geom_point(data = dt, aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj), color = "red")   # The limits argument of length 4 plots a map anywhere in the world: basemap(limits = c(100, 160, -20, 30), bathymetry = TRUE)   # The limits are further expanded when using the data argument:  dt <- data.frame(lon = c(-160, 160, 160, -160), lat = c(80, 80, 60, 60))  if(requireNamespace("ggspatial", quietly = TRUE)) { basemap(data = dt) +   ggspatial::geom_spatial_polygon(data = dt, aes(x = lon, y = lat),     fill = NA, color = "red")  # Rotate: basemap(data = dt, rotate = TRUE) +   ggspatial::geom_spatial_polygon(data = dt, aes(x = lon, y = lat),     fill = NA, color = "red") } #> `geom_polypath()` is deprecated: use `ggplot2::geom_polygon()` with the `subgroup` aesthetic #> `geom_polypath()` is deprecated: use `ggplot2::geom_polygon()` with the `subgroup` aesthetic #> Assuming `crs = 4326` in stat_spatial_identity()   # Alternative: basemap(data = dt, rotate = TRUE) +   geom_polygon(data = transform_coord(dt, rotate = TRUE),     aes(x = lon, y = lat), fill = NA, color = "red")   ## To find UTM coordinates to limit a polar map: basemap(limits = 60, projection.grid = TRUE)   if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # (Arctic shapes require a download in 2.0) basemap(limits = c(2.5e4, -2.5e6, 2e6, -2.5e5), shapefiles = "Arctic")  # Using custom shapefiles (requires download): data(bs_shapes, package = "ggOceanMapsData") basemap(shapefiles = list(land = bs_land))#'  # Premade shapefiles from ggOceanMapsLargeData (requires download): basemap("BarentsSea", bathymetry = TRUE) } # }  # grid.col = NA removes grid lines, rotate = TRUE rotates northwards: basemap(limits = c(-180, -140, 50, 70), grid.col = NA, rotate = TRUE)   # Add axis labels  basemap(limits = c(-140, -105, 20, 40), bathymetry = TRUE) + labs(y = "Latitude", x = "Longitude")   # Remove axis text  basemap(limits = c(0, 60, 68, 80), rotate = TRUE) + theme(axis.title = element_blank(),       axis.text = element_blank(),       axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),       axis.ticks.y = element_blank()       )  # }

title: Calculate distance to the closest land for coordinates — dist2land
text: data Data frame sf object containing geographic coordinates. lon, lat Either names longitude latitude columns data NULL guess longitude /latitude columns data. shapefile Land shape distances calculated. Either character argument referring name pre-made shapefiles shapefile_list, single sf sp polygons object object NULL enable automatic definition land shapes based data. Set "DecimalDegree" default enables great circle distances using s2 features assuming spherical Earth (contrast earlier versions function used flat Earth). proj.coordinate reference system data. bind Logical indicating whether x returned distances (TRUE, default) distances returned vector (FALSE). dist.col name distance column, bind = TRUE. Defaults "ldist". binary Logical indicating whether binary (TRUE = position ocean, FALSE = position land) returned instead distances. Speeds function considerably. verbose Logical indicating whether information process returned messages. Set FALSE make function silent.

title: Calculate distance to the closest land for coordinates — dist2land
text: Returns vector bind = FALSE, otherwise data frame. distances given new column defined dist.col argument. distances kilometers binary = FALSE, otherwise logical (TRUE = position ocean, FALSE = position land).

title: Calculate distance to the closest land for coordinates — dist2land
text: function calculates great circle spherical distances using st_distance function default. function can slow large datasets. want use function remove (wrong) observations reported land, set binary argument TRUE. speeds calculations factor ten.

title: Calculate distance to the closest land for coordinates — dist2land
text: Mikko Vihtakari

title: Calculate distance to the closest land for coordinates — dist2land
code: # Simple example: dt <- data.frame(lon = seq(-20, 80, length.out = 41), lat = 50:90) dt <- dist2land(dt, verbose = FALSE) # \donttest{ qmap(dt, color = ldist) + scale_color_viridis_c()   # Datasets covering the entire Earth seem to work now, except 0,0 lon/lat point lon = deg_to_dd(seq(0,360,30)); lat = c(80,50,20,0,-20,-50,-80)  dt <- data.frame(  lon = rep(lon, length(lat)), lat = rep(lat, each = length(lon)))  qmap(dist2land(dt, verbose = FALSE), color = ldist) +  scale_color_viridis_c()  # } if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ dt <- data.frame(   lon = deg_to_dd(seq(0,360,length.out = 1e3)),    lat = rep(60, 1000))    # The distance calculation is slow for large datasets system.time(dist2land(dt)) # user  system elapsed  # 12.677   0.146  12.849   # binary = TRUE speeds the function up system.time(dist2land(dt, binary = TRUE)) # user  system elapsed  # 1.239   0.120   1.369  } # }

title: Quick map — qmap
text: data Data frame use. x, y, ... Aesthetics passed layer. Longitude latitude columns automatically recognized using guess_coordinate_columns function. geom Character argument specifying geom(s) draw. Defaults "point". alternatives "text" "label". "text" option can also triggered simply mapping variable label (see Examples). limits Map limits. One following: numeric vector length 4: first element defines start longitude, second element end longitude (counter-clockwise), third element minimum latitude, fourth element maximum latitude bounding box. Also accepts sf::st_bbox type named vectors limits order. coordinates can given decimal degrees coordinate units shapefiles used projected map. Produces rectangular map. Latitude limits given min-max order automatically ordered respect requirement. single integer 30 88 -88 -30 produces polar map Arctic Antarctic, respectively. Can omitted data shapefiles defined. shapefiles Either list containing shapefile information character argument referring name pre-made shapefiles shapefile_list. name partially matched. Can omitted limits data defined decimal degrees. crs Coordinate reference system (CRS) map. NULL (default), CRS selected automatically based limits, data, shapefiles. Passed st_crs. Typically integers giving EPGS code easiest. used simultaneously rotate. bathymetry Logical indicating whether bathymetry added map. Functions together bathy.style. See Details. glaciers Logical indicating whether glaciers ice sheets added map. rotate Logical indicating whether projected maps rotated point towards pole relative mid-longitude limit. legends Logical indicating whether legend bathymetry shown. legend.position position ggplot2 legend. See argument name theme. lon.interval, lat.interval Numeric value specifying interval longitude latitude grids. NULL finds reasonable defaults depending limits. bathy.style Character (plots bathymetry; list alternatives Details) NULL ("raster_binned_blues" bathymetry = TRUE) defining bathymetry style. Partially matched, can abbreviated, used control bathymetry plotting together bathymetry. See Details. downsample Integer defining downsampling rate raster bathymetries. value 0 (default) downsample, 1 skips every second row, 2 every second third. See geom_stars bathy.alpha Transparency parameter bathymetry fill color. See scale_alpha. land.col, gla.col, grid.col Character code specifying color land, glaciers, grid lines, respectively. Use NA remove grid lines. land.border.col, gla.border.col, bathy.border.col Character code specifying color border line land, glacier, bathymetry shapes. land.size, gla.size, bathy.size, grid.size Numeric value specifying width border line land, glacier bathymetry shapes well grid lines, respectively. Use LS function specific width pt. See Details. base_size Base size parameter ggplot. See ggtheme. projection.grid Logical indicating whether coordinate grid show projected coordinates instead decimal degree values. Useful define limits large maps polar regions. expand.factor Expansion factor map limits. Can used zoom (decrease value 1) (increase value 1) automatically (data) limited maps. Defaults 1, means outermost data points located boundaries plotting region. verbose Logical indicating whether information projection guessed column names returned messages. Set FALSE make function silent.

what: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
text: Fetches gridded bathymetry data demand OGC Web   Coverage Service (WCS) returns bathyRaster object   compatible basemap vector_bathymetry.   Two sources currently supported: "emodnet" (~115 m European   waters) "etopo" (1 arc-minute / ~1.85 km global).

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
code: wcs_bathymetry(   limits,   source = "emodnet",   coverage = NULL,   cache_dir = NULL,   force = FALSE,   max_area_deg2 = NULL,   tile_size_deg = NULL,   downsample = 0,   timeout = 60,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
text: limits Numeric vector length 4 giving bounding box decimal degrees c(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax). source Character. WCS source query. One "emodnet" (European waters, high-res) "etopo" (global, 1 arc-minute). Partial matching applies. coverage Character. Override default coverage source (e.g. "emodnet__mean_2022" 2022-vintage EMODnet DTM). NULL uses source default. cache_dir Character. Directory downloaded GeoTIFFs cached. Defaults getOption("ggOceanMaps.datapath") tempdir(). force Logical. TRUE, re-download even cached file exists. max_area_deg2 Numeric. Maximum bounding-box area (degree-squared) allowed function errors. Guards accidentally downloading large area. NULL uses source default (50 EMODnet, 2000 ETOPO). Bounding boxes larger source's single-request cap split tiles mosaicked automatically – see Details. tile_size_deg Numeric. Edge length (degrees) largest single-request tile. NULL uses source default (3 EMODnet, 30 ETOPO). EMODnet reads 8-byte doubles internally 4-degree tile already exceeds ~98 MB read cap; ETOPO much coarser larger tiles fine. downsample Non-negative integer. Number grid cells skip requesting raster. 0 (default) keeps source resolution; 1 requests half rows columns; n requests every (n+1)-th cell. Downsampling performed WCS server, reducing transfer size memory use. timeout Positive numeric. Minimum HTTP timeout seconds. larger existing global timeout option respected. verbose Logical. Print download progress informational messages.

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
text: bathyRaster object: list elements raster   (read_stars object positive depth values)   depth.invervals (length-2 numeric range).

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
text: EMODnet serves European-waters bathymetric DTM   ~115 m native resolution (~0.00104 deg) EPSG:4326 GeoTIFF format.   1x1 deg tile around North Sea ~4 MB; 5x5 deg tile   ~100 MB hit server's ~98 MB read cap. Coverage European   regional seas (~-36 43 lon, ~15 90 lat). ETOPO (ETOPO1 Ice Surface, served NOAA NCEI) global   1 arc-minute (~1.85 km) topo-bathy grid EPSG:4326. Useful EMODnet   coverage. NCEI returns GeoTIFF inside multipart/related MIME   envelope; wcs_bathymetry() extracts binary part transparently. handle bounding boxes larger source's single-request cap,   wcs_bathymetry() splits request tiles   tile_size_deg per axis, caches tile, mosaicks via   GDAL virtual raster. tile cached independently subsequent   overlapping requests reuse already disk. returned object bathyRaster (class returned   raster_bathymetry depths = NULL) can   slotted directly basemap(shapefiles = list(bathy = ...))   passed vector_bathymetry. Citation requirements: EMODnet bathymetry published CC-  must cited used figures   (https://emodnet.ec.europa.eu/en/bathymetry). ETOPO1 also requires   citation (Amante & Eakins 2009, NOAA NGDC; see   https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/etopo-global-relief-model).

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
text: Mikko Vihtakari

text: EMODnet — bathy.style = "wcs_emodnet_blues" ("wemb") / "wcs_emodnet_grays" ("wemg"). ~115 m European-waters bathymetry. ETOPO1 NOAA NCEI — bathy.style = "wcs_etopo_blues" ("wceb") / "wcs_etopo_grays" ("wceg"). ~1.85 km global topo-bathy. Use EMODnet coverage region. Bounding boxes outside source’s coverage error cleanly pointer right alternative. Large boxes tiled mosaicked automatically. Tiles cached getOption("ggOceanMaps.datapath"). vector_land() extracts land polygon bathyRaster produced raster_bathymetry(). Pairs vector_bathymetry() build--shapefile workflows. New website Bathymetry article covering bathymetry sources. New website Adding graphical elements article covering ocean-current arrows (velocity quivers schematic “Figure 1” arrows) pie charts maps via scatterpie::geom_scatterpie(). title: ggOceanMaps 3.0.0
text: Fixed land clipped early projected (polar-stereographic) maps decimal-degree limits, e.g. basemap(c(-20, 30, 50, 70)) cut northern Norway. land clip boundary now densified back-projecting WGS84. Fixed antimeridian / wide-span land clipping routing clip projected map CRS, fixing wrong land basemap(c(120, -120, 60, 80)) topology crash rotated antimeridian data input. Fixed crash (st_cast() degenerate GEOMETRYCOLLECTION) custom vector_land() layers map limits. Fixed rotated antimeridian maps drawing land, e.g. basemap(c(100, -120, -12, -57), rotate = TRUE) basemap(c(40, -70, -37, 40), rotate = TRUE). landmass crossing rotated antimeridian (even -screen, Antarctica) “unwrapped” degenerate >180-degree polygon projection, made ggplot2 skip whole land layer. polygons now split along seam projection (issue #44). Fixed default bathymetry heavily downsampled. raster warped onto coarse ~256-cell grid regardless source resolution, e.g. basemap(60, bathymetry = TRUE) lost detail. warp now keeps source resolution default; use downsample argument reduce . Fixed basemap("DecimalDegree", bathymetry = TRUE) (whole-world set glaciers = TRUE) collapsing bathymetry single row equator. Clipping global raster exact -180..180 longitude box returned degenerate boundary; whole-world decimal-degree limits now pulled just inside antimeridian clipping. Fixed WCS bathy.style ("wemb" / "wceb", etc.) failing “st_transform applied object class ‘logical’” combined explicitly named premade shapefile set, e.g. basemap(..., bathy.style = "wemb", shapefiles = "Svalbard"). -demand bathymetry now fetched case . Fixed WCS bathymetry leaving white gaps around edges projected maps (e.g. Svalbard Europe CRS). coverage now requested full projected, expand-factor-padded map area rather raw decimal-degree limits, fills whole panel. WCS downsample now applied remote service, reducing transfer size memory use. Raster cropping preserves downloaded cell resolution longer applies second implicit reduction plotting. WCS downloads now use validated temporary files atomic cache updates. Invalid cache entries replaced automatically, multipart responses split using complete MIME boundary rather generic byte sequence can occur inside GeoTIFF.
